description:
You are an IT-support technician working on ticket #9901.  
    The user reports “My machine can’t even reach itself; I think the loop-back interface is down.”  
    Your job is to run ONE (and only one) network-diagnostic command from the terminal to prove whether the
    local TCP/IP stack is functioning.  When you are done you must save the console output to a
    log file so our ticketing system can attach it to the case.

    Exact requirements the automated grader will check:

    1. Create a new directory  
       /home/user/it_support_logs  
       if it does not already exist.

    2. Inside that directory create a UTF-8 text file named  
       /home/user/it_support_logs/ticket_9901_ping_localhost.log

    3. Capture the *complete terminal output* of a **single** diagnostic command that tests basic
       network connectivity with the loop-back address 127.0.0.1 and write it verbatim to the log file.  
       • Any additional shell prompts, commentary, or extra commands are **NOT** allowed in the file.  
       • The command should only produce one round-trip attempt so that the output is short.

    4. The log file must contain (anywhere in the file) **all** of the following four textual
       fragments; the grader will search for them as plain substrings:
          a) “PING 127.0.0.1”  
          b) “1 packets transmitted”  
          c) “1 received”  
          d) “0% packet loss”

    5. File permissions must allow the instructor’s account to read the file
       (e.g. mode 0644 or more permissive).

    6. No other files should be created or modified.

    Once these steps are complete the ticket can be considered fixed.
truth:
The grader will verify that:
    • The path /home/user/it_support_logs/ticket_9901_ping_localhost.log exists.  
    • The file is non-empty.  
    • It contains all four of the required substrings exactly as listed (case-sensitive).  
    • No extra commands or commentary appear before or after the captured command output
      (the grader will reject lines beginning with “$”, “#”, or shell prompts).  
    • Only one command’s output is present; multiple PING blocks or unrelated command
      output will fail the test.
name: task_000000_0568dece
